topic: A tenant receives an eviction notice
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white envelope landed on the worn welcome mat with a soft, almost insulting thud. Elias picked it up, his fingers tracing the thick paper as if it were contaminated. His breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that seemed to catch in his chest. He unfolded the document, and the stark, official language swam before his eyes. "Notice of Termination of Tenancy." His gaze flickered from the date to the clause detailing the required move-out timeline. A slow, creeping coldness began to spread from his stomach outwards. He reread the section three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. He stood up abruptly, the cheap particleboard of the coffee table scraping against the floor. He walked to the window, staring out at the neighboring apartment complex, the familiar view suddenly feeling alien and hostile.

[story 2]

Sarah was midway through watering her basil plants when the mail slot rattled. She pulled out the thick, cream-colored paper, and the contents caused her hand to tremble visibly. Her initial reaction was paralysis; she stood frozen, the watering can halfway to the soil, the water sloshing precariously. She lowered the paper slowly, her eyes scanning the printed text. A strange, hollow sensation settled over her, heavy and undeniable. She pressed the paper against her forehead, closing her eyes tight. When she opened them again, the details of the notice seemed sharper, more defined, each line a hammer blow against the quiet afternoon. She let out a small, involuntary sound—a soft gasp—and dropped the watering can onto the patio stones with a clatter.

[story 3]

Mark was sorting through old receipts when he spotted the offending letter tucked beneath a utility bill. He picked it up, his thumb repeatedly brushing the edges of the stiff paper. His jaw tightened, a muscle ticking visibly beneath his skin. He read the opening paragraph, and for a moment, the room seemed to tilt. His eyes widened slightly, pupils dilating as he absorbed the implications of the written decree. He let the letter slip from his grasp, and it fluttered to the floor, landing near his worn slippers. He took a deep, ragged breath, the air tasting suddenly thin. He paced three steps toward the kitchen counter, his movements jerky and uncoordinated, before stopping dead still, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the counter.
